citation: Mann JB, Mayhew JL, Dos Santos ML, Dawes JJ, Signorile JF. Momentum, rather than velocity, is a more effective measure of improvements in Division IA football player performance. Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research. 2022;36(2):551-557.
abstract: This longitudinal Division I football study examined whether momentum, calculated as body mass multiplied by average velocity, better reflects career improvement than sprint or agility velocity alone. Across 15 years of team testing, complete four-year data were analyzed for the 40-yard sprint, pro-agility shuttle, and L-drill. Velocity improved mainly after year 1 and then plateaued, while body mass increased across all years. Momentum increased significantly across all years for all tests, suggesting that football-specific development is missed when staff only track time or velocity.
tldr: For college football players, sprint and agility times may flatten after the first year, but momentum can keep improving as athletes add useful mass without losing speed.
methods: Retrospective longitudinal analysis of one NCAA Division I program's winter testing data. Players completed standardized body mass, 40-yard sprint, pro-agility, and L-drill testing after a six-week winter conditioning period. Velocity was calculated from test distance divided by time, and momentum was calculated as body mass times velocity. Repeated-measures analyses compared body mass, velocity, and momentum across four playing years for athletes with complete consecutive data.
findings: Velocity improved from year 1 to later years but generally showed no meaningful improvement from years 2 through 4. Body mass increased significantly across all playing years. Momentum increased across every year for the 40-yard sprint, pro-agility, and L-drill, making it a more sensitive indicator of football-relevant physical progress than time alone.
limitations: Data came from a single program and were not separated by position group, so standards may not transfer directly across rosters or roles. Attrition reduced the full-career sample from the larger roster pool, and change-of-direction tests used average velocity rather than segment-specific acceleration, deceleration, or collision data.
practicalImplications: Baylor staff can add momentum calculations to existing timing and body mass reports with no new hardware. Use it alongside raw times so bigger athletes are not penalized for maintaining speed while adding functional mass, especially for collision roles.
athleteDev: Momentum gives a clearer development story for athletes whose body mass targets are part of the plan. It can support position-specific conversations about adding mass, preserving speed, and showing progress when stopwatch improvements slow.
rtp: For return-to-play, momentum should not replace clinical or tissue-readiness criteria, but it can help compare an athlete's post-injury speed-mass profile with his own pre-injury baseline and position expectations.

citation: Lacome M, Simpson B, Broad N, Buchheit M. Monitoring players' readiness using predicted heart rate responses to football drills. International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance. 2018;13(10):1273-1280.
abstract: This elite soccer study tested whether individual models built from GPS and heart-rate data could predict the heart-rate cost of small-sided games and whether the gap between predicted and actual heart rate could monitor fitness or readiness. Ten elite field players wore 5-Hz GPS/accelerometers and heart-rate monitors across a season. Individual multivariate models predicted heart rate from external-load variables, adjusted for heat, and the actual-minus-predicted difference was compared with submaximal run heart-rate responses and seasonal fitness trends. Predicted and actual heart rate were very largely correlated, and changes in the heart-rate delta tracked changes in submaximal running heart rate.
tldr: Player-specific expected-HR models can turn normal practice drills into a daily readiness signal when interpreted as trends, not single noisy data points.
methods: Ten elite French soccer players were monitored during the 2016-2017 season. Small-sided games and a 4-minute submaximal 12 km/h run were analyzed with GPS, accelerometer-derived load variables, heart rate, and heat-index adjustment. Stepwise individual regression models used external-load variables such as total distance, high-speed distance, velocity load, force load, and mechanical work to predict drill heart rate. The actual-minus-predicted heart-rate delta was validated against submaximal run heart rate and month-to-month seasonal changes.
findings: Predicted and actual drill heart rate were very largely correlated (r = 0.78 +/- 0.04), with force load and mechanical work explaining more of the response than distance alone. Within-player changes in heart-rate delta correlated largely with changes in submaximal run heart rate (r = 0.66). The delta improved from preseason into early season, consistent with expected fitness gains.
limitations: The sample was small and elite-soccer specific. Models need enough historical data, roughly 60 data points or 6-8 weeks, so they are not immediately useful for new players. Heart-rate strap compliance, contact-related signal errors, session timing, cardiac drift, and GPS sampling limitations can all affect estimates.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, the concept is more important than the exact soccer drills: model each athlete's expected internal response to known external work, then flag when heart rate is unusually high or low for that work. Mechanical work, accelerations, decelerations, and force-load style variables should be included rather than relying only on distance.
athleteDev: This approach can help separate conditioning progress from simple workload exposure. Athletes who show lower-than-expected heart-rate cost for comparable football movement demands may be adapting, while persistent higher-than-expected cost may suggest fatigue, illness risk, heat strain, or conditioning needs.
rtp: In RTP progressions, expected-versus-actual heart-rate responses can provide a field-based check on whether the athlete is tolerating position-specific drills efficiently, especially when paired with submaximal run tests and subjective symptoms.

citation: Buchheit M, Sandua M, Gray A, Hader K, Monnot D, Volante J, Delafosse F. Monitoring the reconditioning of the injured football player with field-based measures: A case study following ACL reconstruction. Aspetar Sports Medicine Journal. 2023:374-381.
abstract: This case study describes field-based metabolic reconditioning and monitoring for an elite professional soccer player after ACL reconstruction with associated meniscal and ligament injury. The authors outline off-leg conditioning, progressive running, change-of-direction exposure, football-specific drills, and return-to-train integration. Monitoring included GPS-derived locomotor load, peak intensity, mechanical work, submaximal run heart rate, and GPS-embedded inertial measures of running mechanics and step impulse symmetry. The player was projected to return to full team training without restrictions about 7.5 months after injury.
tldr: Post-ACL reconditioning should preserve fitness early, restore football-specific peak demands later, and use pre-injury baselines to guide running, COD, and asymmetry decisions.
methods: Single-player applied case report from Lille OSC. The athlete was a 23-year-old elite central defender who sustained ACL rupture with anterolateral ligament, MCL, lateral meniscus, and suspected ramp-lesion involvement and underwent hamstring autograft ACL reconstruction plus anterolateral ligament reconstruction and meniscal repair. The paper describes phase-based off-leg, AlterG, overground running, COD, individual football, and team-training progressions using heart-rate, GPS, mechanical-work, peak-intensity, submaximal-run, and inertial stride metrics.
findings: The case emphasized that aggressive but controlled off-leg conditioning can limit deconditioning before running is available. A 4-minute submaximal run suggested the athlete had lost only about 1 km/h of maximal aerobic speed at the start of on-field rehab, and run-based plus bike/ErgoSki HIIT helped return fitness toward pre-injury levels. Peak mechanical-work intensities were harder to reproduce in individual rehab than in team or match contexts, and step impulse asymmetries provided useful day-to-day information during return to running.
limitations: This is a single elite soccer case, not a controlled trial. The injury included meniscal repair and associated ligament findings, so timelines and progressions are not generalizable to all ACL reconstructions. The approach depends on strong pre-injury data, multidisciplinary staffing, GPS/inertial analytics, and individualized interpretation.
practicalImplications: Baylor should preserve cardiovascular fitness early with low-limb-load conditioning, then progressively restore position-specific running, acceleration, deceleration, COD, and peak mechanical-work exposures. Maintaining pre-injury baselines for submax runs, GPS loads, and running mechanics is valuable because those references become rehab targets after injury.
athleteDev: The case supports treating long-term injury time as a development window, not only a protection period. Staff can build aerobic capacity, tissue tolerance, running mechanics, and movement confidence while respecting graft, meniscus, and strength milestones.
rtp: RTP should progress from clinical criteria to controlled field exposure to chaotic team demands. Submaximal heart-rate response, peak intensity, mechanical work, and limb asymmetry trends can help decide when to advance running speed, COD intensity, partial team training, and full unrestricted participation.

citation: Frank BS, Hackney AC, Battaglini CL, Blackburn T, Marshall SW, Clark M, Padua DA. Movement profile influences systemic stress and biomechanical resilience to high training load exposure. Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport. 2019;22(1):35-41.
abstract: This cross-sectional cohort study examined whether movement quality influences systemic stress and mechanical loading before and after high training load exposure. Forty-three physically active college-aged female field or court sport athletes were classified as excellent or poor movers using Landing Error Scoring System criteria. Participants completed five cycles of treadmill running at 100-120% ventilatory threshold plus repeated jump-landings. Serum cortisol and jump-landing vertical ground reaction force were measured before and after exercise. Poor movers showed higher overall cortisol and higher vertical ground reaction force before and after the high-load bout.
tldr: Poor movement profiles may make the same training load more mechanically and systemically costly, even when athletes have similar fitness.
methods: Female field or court sport athletes were grouped by LESS-derived landing profile. After baseline cortisol sampling and jump-landing force-plate testing, they completed five cycles of 5 minutes of treadmill running at 100-120% ventilatory threshold plus 10 jump-landings from a 30-cm box. Researchers measured serum cortisol with ELISA and analyzed dominant-limb vertical ground reaction force waveforms before and after the high-training-load protocol.
findings: The poor and excellent movement groups did not differ in cortisol change response to the high-load bout, but the poor group had higher overall cortisol across time (d = 0.85). Both groups increased cortisol after the protocol. Poor movers had greater vertical ground reaction force across much of stance before and after exercise, and after high load they showed increased force across a larger portion of stance than excellent movers.
limitations: The sample was healthy college-aged women with field or court sport history, so findings may not transfer directly to male football players. The study used one acute lab protocol and a sagittal-dominant jump-landing task, without direct inflammation, blood glucose, or sport-specific cutting/contact measures. It is associative rather than proof that changing movement quality lowers cortisol or injury risk.
practicalImplications: Movement quality may influence how costly a training load is. Baylor staff should view poor landing or stiff movement strategies as possible load amplifiers and consider integrating movement-quality screens with GPS, force-plate, wellness, and recovery data during high-load blocks.
athleteDev: Corrective movement training may improve more than aesthetics of landing. Better trunk, hip, and knee strategies could reduce mechanical loading and potentially improve resilience during heavy training phases.
rtp: For RTP athletes, passing a workload target is not enough if the movement strategy remains stiff or high-impact. Pair workload progression with landing and deceleration mechanics so the athlete is not accumulating excessive force exposure while fitness returns.

citation: Brophy RH, Baker JC, Crain JM, Herzog MM, Stollberg B, Wojtys EM, Mack CD. MRI findings associated with anterior cruciate ligament tears in National Football League athletes. Orthopaedic Journal of Sports Medicine. 2023;11(6):23259671231169190.
abstract: This NFL cross-sectional imaging study described concomitant MRI findings after ACL tears. From 314 ACL injuries during the 2015-2019 NFL seasons, 191 complete primary-injury MRI scans without prior ACL reconstruction were reviewed by fellowship-trained musculoskeletal radiologists. Bone bruises were present in 94.8% of knees, and 89% had additional meniscal, ligamentous, or cartilage injury. Meniscal tears occurred in 70%, additional ligament injury in 71%, and cartilage damage in 49%. Most ACL tears did not involve direct contact to the injured limb, and imaging patterns varied by mechanism.
tldr: NFL ACL tears are rarely isolated; staff should expect and plan around meniscus, MCL/other ligament, cartilage, and bone-bruise findings when forecasting rehab.
methods: Researchers identified NFL ACL tears from 2015 through 2019, located complete acute MRI scans in the league imaging system, excluded reinjuries after prior ACL reconstruction, and reviewed 191 primary ACL injury MRIs. Two fellowship-trained musculoskeletal radiologists abstracted ACL tear features, bone bruising, meniscal injury, cartilage pathology, and concomitant ligament injury. MRI data were linked with video-derived mechanism classifications where available and analyzed with reliability statistics and Fisher exact tests.
findings: Bone bruising was nearly universal, especially lateral tibial plateau and lateral femoral condyle. Meniscal tears were common, more often lateral than medial, with radial and complex lateral tears frequent; 30% had both medial and lateral tears. Additional ligament injury was present in 71%, most often MCL involvement. Cartilage damage appeared in 49%, including full-thickness defects in 25%. Direct contact injuries were more associated with MCL tear and MPFL injury, while noncontact patterns more often showed MCL sprain and medial tibial plateau bruising.
limitations: MRI scans were available for 61% of league ACL injuries, and missing scans could differ from included cases. NFL athletes may not generalize to college, high school, or other sports. MRI findings were not confirmed intraoperatively, treatment and follow-up outcomes were unavailable, scanner protocols varied, and reviewer agreement was moderate overall.
practicalImplications: When a Baylor football athlete tears an ACL, early planning should assume a high probability of concomitant pathology until imaging and surgical findings prove otherwise. Meniscus, cartilage, MCL, MPFL, and bone-bruise status should shape load progression, communication with coaches, and expectations for timelines.
athleteDev: The study reinforces the importance of knee health history and mechanism detail in athlete profiling. Preventive work should not focus on the ACL in isolation; deceleration, valgus control, contact preparedness, and tissue-capacity strategies must account for the whole knee.
rtp: RTP timelines after ACL injury should be individualized around associated damage, not just graft maturation. Meniscal repair, cartilage defects, MCL injury, bone bruising, and mechanism-specific patterns may all influence weight-bearing, running progression, cutting exposure, and risk discussions.
